sentences.text: --removed from that transport.
Well, Leo told me to go back to my room and hide under the bunk in the roof of the building.
Just when people come and look for you, you're not there.
And they all know you're coming back.
So don't worry about it.
And he did different things with [?
Milich ?] and my mother.
But it all came out.
Because he was told-- you know, the Jewish organization was organizing these transports.
That's the invidious thing that it was the Jewish management that was made to do it.
And then when they were not allowed, when they said-- when there was some resistance to doing it, off they went to Auschwitz, you see.
So that September transport was a [NON-ENGLISH] transport.
Not only that, I've been puzzling about it for many, many years.
It was called the Heydrich [NON-ENGLISH] transport.
Now, the Heydrich assassination took place a year and a half before the transport.
How come that the Germans should remember a period in September 1943 when it's a year and a half since the assassination of Heydrich?
But it was called the Heydrich [NON-ENGLISH] transport, a punishment transport.
And that's the last we see of them.
And we stay in Theresienstadt.
And I'm very friendly with Fela and Binya and this group of six, which was like a kernel inside-- inside the thing.
And I told you that Fela sort of walked with an effeminate-- effeminate-- and-- walk.
And we would-- I-- I used to skive off school.
I had a problem with our teacher, [?
Madrich, ?] who was in charge of our room.
Because I skived off school.
I played football.
I had all kinds of things.
I didn't go to school.
And the excuse I gave was you organize me to learn a trade, I'll go.
But I'm not going to start learning history.
I got once a punishment.
I had to read the whole book and memorize the whole book off by heart by nighttime.
Thin book, not a big book.
So I memorized it.
And then [?
Madrich, ?] said, who else would like to volunteer to memorize this book?
And I'll give him an examination tomorrow night.
Like, tomorrow.
The boy who-- who volunteered is Otto Jakubowicz.
He lives in Sheffield.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And Otto went through the book and answered all the questions.
I had three mistakes in the whole book.
But Otto had none.
And I was told, you see?
You didn't study it properly.
Now, this [?
Schmudler ?] was trying to do me down.
And I was sort of-- I was a naughty boy.
I was a problem boy to him.
Because I wasn't going to school, and I wasn't listening to what he was telling me to do.
He had a sort of point system going in the room.
And he would punish me and pick on me by not giving me points.
Schmudler ?] survived the war.
And even now that we are-- he's much older than me-- he's in-- in his late '70s, we still don't like each other.
[LAUGHTER]
No.
I never was bar mitzvah, no.
Well, there was very much Zionist education.
And there were books.
And you could read by yourself, which I did do.
But-- and I was extremely good at debating.
So in the room, if there was any debate going, I-- I would always be in the forefront.
And in fact, with this kernel, we were sort of the people who read books.
And-- And we sort of discussed things well into the night, you know, as boys will.
And one day, in fact, before the December transport, about a couple of weeks, [?
Schmudler ?] gave us a lecture, which actually impressed me no end.
He talked about homosexuality.
And I remember the lecture.
Actually, I talked to people in my room.
And I talked to [?
Schmudler ?] about this lecture.
None of them remember it.
I seem to be only one to remember it.
And I think I-- as he was giving us the lecture about homosexuality, I thought he was talking to me.
Because Fela, we use to sort of laugh at him that he was-- well, you couldn't laugh at him, because he'd give you one.
But-- because he was-- he looked effeminate.
He wasn't effeminate.
But he just sort of looked effeminate.
The view of homosexuality in those days-- and I remember the lecture completely like now.
Because I felt it was aimed at me and at these [NON-ENGLISH] that we had together, these six boys.
Because [?
Schmudler ?] thought there was something going on, you know, we were masturbating or something.
Actually, nothing ever went on.
We really had a sort of-- we were meeting on an intellectual plane.
Perhaps it could have developed between us, because we were immature or we weren't fed enough.
I don't know why.
But there was nothing that really went on.
But I felt that [?
Schmudler ?] was talking to me because of this Fela.
And the view, of course, was that homosexuality is something you can get used to, that people will lead you into it, and then you will be like that for life.
So you must stay a virgin, you know.
That-- that was the message.
I mean, that was the view in 1943.
And these were modern teachers.
This-- this was the modern view.
Meanwhile, Leo gets told that he has lost his protectsia.
And next time, we are all going.
He's going, his wife, his wife's mother, the widow of [?
Dr.
Camp ?] and us.
Whatever the next transport is, the whole family has lost all the protectsia.
You Know, we've-- we are on the list, whatever happens.
So comes the December transport.
And we are on the first 2,500.
And they are all on the second one.
And now we arrive, about three days later, in Auschwitz.
Terrible, terrible journey.
The worst of the journey was that you want to go to the toilet, and you can't, because you are in a sealed compartment.
You know, later on during the death marches, as they were already shipping us in open wagons.
So you could sort of-- people would hold you.
And you could do your business on the other side of the wagon.
But with a closed wagon, you couldn't do anything.
Nobody thought of taking a pen knife, you know, to make a hole in the floor of the wagon.
No water, so there was thirst.
And there were dead bodies.
People died in the compartment, old people.
Milich ?] was with a girlfriend.
I mean, they found each other in-- in the close proximity of-- of this cramped space.
And-- and they were necking.
They were-- for three days, they-- [LAUGHS] they had some sort of sexual experience.
She was a very pretty girl.
Well, he was right next to me.
They were right next to me.
[LAUGHS] And my mother was shattered.
She hardly spoke.
It was dark.
Even during the daytime, it was dark, smelly, dirty.
And lots of times, you stand in sidings.
You're not even traveling.
But we arrive at night.
I was watching my brother necking with this girlfriend.
I wasn't imagining-- look, I was a boy of 14 at the time.
I'd steal anything.
I'd go anywhere, you know.
I didn't have this sort of introspection into camp and being a prisoner and all this.
You're prisoner so long as you're kept a prisoner.
But if there's an opportunity, you run away.
So you become like a [? lag, ?] you know, in a-- in a prison.
You-- you get that sort of mentality.
And that's the sort of mentality that I had.
I never had this mumbo-jumbo about psychology that people talk about.
I don't remember that.
We arrive at this quite a fantastic scene, at night time.
Suddenly there's light, blinding light.
Although, it's night time.
But it's-- it's like this light, you know?
It burns your eyes suddenly.
And shouting, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
And there are these people in striped pajamas with sticks.
And they are laying into people, you know, to jump out.
So [?
Milich ?] and I, you know, we help-- we help mother to jump.
And they say, men this side, women this side, into the trucks, into the trucks, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
And you look at a row of yellow lights over the fences.
You can it's fences.
You can't see the wire.
But you can see the-- the lights, you see.
And you can see rows and rows of lights.
And then you could see rows of lights going away from you at right angles.
And on the other side of the sidings, if you duck under the-- the train, you can see exactly the same.
All you see is this row of lights 100 yards or so away.
And then at right angles, there are these dividing lights.
So you see fences, you know?
So you start talking to these striped pajama people.
And they speak Yiddish.
You say, so-- so where are we?
So they said, Auschwitz.
Where is Auschwitz?
Poland.
They don't say southern Poland, Galicia.
It's-- I don't know geography in Poland.
And in this confusion, I lose both-- both [?
Milich ?] and my mom.
I'm on my own.
So they say, throw away your rucksack.
Because it-- it's all labeled.
And it'll be given to you in the camp.
But I already knew what was happening in the [NON-ENGLISH] So a fellow tears the rucksack off me.
You're going without the rucksack.
And I had a sort of side small case where we-- where we had some food.
So I said, let me take this, you know, I said to him in Yiddish.
So he looks at me and says, all right, onto the lorry.
I get on the lorry.
We get to the sauna, the baths.
And they all say, you're a special treatment.
You're not-- you're going into a family camp.
So you'll meet your mother.
Even the prisoners tell you this.
And I don't find [?
Milich, ?] because he's amongst the men.
But I find my mother.
And by now, it's way into the night.
We get into this separate camp, which is the family camp, so called.
[NON-ENGLISH] was the word for it.
[NON-ENGLISH] means compound.
So there were-- in Auschwitz Birkenau, there were compounds of 10,000. 10,000 consisted of 30 barracks and two kitchen barracks.
Then there were four stalls barracks at the end of the camp.
The kitchens were at the gate of the camp.
And the camp was really fenced in.
They were the fences that went at right angles to the fences at the railway yards.
We know nothing of the crematoria yet and so on.
But we're being tattooed.
And I'm with my mother.
And there are some of the boys.
One of the Birkenau boys is five numbers away from me, Harry Goldenberg.
He's recently had a stroke, poor chap.
We're all being tattooed.
So the man who-- the-- the-- the-- the Jewish prisoners who are tattooing, and my mother is ashen seeing that I'm going to be tattooed.
She doesn't know where [?
Milich ?] is.
We don't see him.
She's not yet tattooed, but I'm going to be tattooed.
And he says, don't worry.
I won't hurt him.
And he does it very lightly.
So with me, the-- the beginning numbers are sort of quite faint.
Can you see the one six is quite faint.
And I think Harry Goldberg is 169072.
Can you see the 1 and the 6 are faint?
And then the nine is a little bit heavier?
But even the rest of it isn't very heavy.
Now, it didn't hurt at all.
He's doing this.
And they sort of wipe it away and look at it as it's being done.
And the SS supervisor looks at it.
And he says to the-- says to the prisoner, strike it out and start again.
You know, it's not good enough because the 1 and the 6 are too faint.
So the man sort of talks to him, German, I understand, sort of confidentiality.
You know, he takes him aside.
And he takes out a bottle of vodka.
And he gives him a bottle of vodka.
And he says, look, it's just a kid.
It'll be all right anyway.
And the SS man says, OK.
That's the first instance of that sort of altruistic help from grownups who see children being murdered daily, never tattoo children.
And here he is tattooing a child.
So he's saying to the mother, don't worry.
I won't hurt him.
Now, when I think about my mother and how she behaved sort of towards me, regarding me as a grownup when I wasn't a grownup-- I mean, I was 14.
But 14 is not a grownup.
I mean, I was 16 after the war, and I wasn't a grownup.
Although, I was mature beyond my years.
But in many other ways, I was the same adolescent as any-- anyone else.
I mean, then it dawned on us.
And people started telling us.
The family camp already had the people from September were in this camp, you see.
So we met the other Czech people.
And we, sort of little boys, 14, are allowed to go with our mothers to the-- to the same thing, to the-- to the same barrack.
Barrack number seven I go to.
There's a woman crying bitterly there with a smaller boy than me.
I mean, I was small for my age.
But this was a smaller boy.
I don't know her.
And of course, as soon as someone's in trouble, you know, my mother makes a beeline for this lady.
Who should it be?
It's Mrs.
Edelstein, the wife of the [NON-ENGLISH] elders-- they're from [NON-ENGLISH]-- with her son Arieh.
And all kinds of people come.
And this woman is crying.
Her husband was in the same train, apparently, she knew, and was taken into solitary confinement in Auschwitz but not into the family camp.
So he really had special treatment.
So all sorts of people come to her and this and that.
And it's nighttime.
We go to sleep.
Next morning, it's December, you see.
We arrived December the 18th or December 9, December 21st. 18th, we departed.
No, 15th we departed.
We arrived 18th-- 18th of December, bitterly cold day.
Again, the doors open.
And there is a man dressed in a sort of military uniform.
But he isn't in an SS uniform.
He's got sort of red and yellow sort of badges.
And he's got this German writing.
It's says [GERMAN].
He struts about in high, high riding boots, britches, very smartly dressed, clean shaven.
You know, Yul Brynner, that sort of thing.
And he's hitting people.
He's shouting.
He's screaming like mad.
And he's hitting people.
[GERMAN]-- [GERMAN] meant counting, you see.
So we all file out.
And you know, a kid is quick.
You know.
When he appears with a stick, I sort of say to mother, come this way.
You know.
Your reactions are-- although it's cold and it's bitter-- oh, incidentally, the children were allowed to keep their clothes and this little knapsack that I had.
With the food.
I could take into-- into the camp.
So I saved that.
We had a couple of days, some food.
And it's [INAUDIBLE].
But then-- I sort of remember these memories as pictures, you know?
Like arriving, I remember my thought.
I thought to myself this is an extraordinary picture, you know, this scene.
I'll never see anything like this again.
And I said to myself, you must remember everything that's happening.
And then somebody shoves you and says, run.
So you forget what you're thinking, you know?
Because your instincts take over.
And I don't know whether the mind plays tricks on you when you're young or something.
But the fact that I felt that I had to observe everything sort of wiped out this thing of being afraid.
Because I don't remember being afraid.
It was very frightening.
But I-- I don't remember being afraid.
I remember trying to orientate and know where I am.
You know, where's mother?
I always lost mother in crises.
She was always gone someplace, always looking after other people.
We get to block number seven.
This woman's crying, Mrs.
Edelstein.
After the [NON-ENGLISH], we file back in.
She's inconsolable.
And my mother says to me, there's a block number 30-- no, 29.
Because we were seven.
The outside was looking down the camp from the gate.
The odd numbers were on the left.
Even numbers are on the right.
Number 29, go to number 29 and take this boy Arieh with you.
In block 29, you will find Freddy Hirsch.
Tell him I sent you.
He knows you.
And tell him he should keep you there, both of you.
And go.
So I went.
And this boy was a bit of a wimp, actually.
He didn't want to leave his mother and so on.
So I said, look, your mother is in trouble.
My mother's taking care of her.
Don't worry about it.
You're told to go, go.
We go.
And Freddy knows me.
And Freddy knows Arieh Edelstein.
Because Edelstein was the [NON-ENGLISH].
And through the meetings of the Judenrat he knew Arieh as well.
So I-- so Freddy laughs.
You know, it's a big joke.
You know, I'm arriving with Arieh.
And he says, ah, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
You're already here.
[LAUGHS] Or like, at last, you're here.
You know?
I've been waiting for you.
[LAUGHS] And everybody's laughing, you know?
Because-- and this was his method to prevent the children from being afraid.
And it was infectious.
Was it easy to go from one block to another?
Well, there was a lot of snow.
And-- and actually, it was all mud.
It was terribly muddy.
So it was a family camp.
And if you didn't ask questions-- that's what my mother said.
Don't ask any questions, go.
If you started standing around and saying, can I go, people would say, no, you can't.
You've got to go back.
Well, I dragged this Arieh there.
So Freddy said, well, you're going to have a job as the stoker of this oven.
We've got some boxes, which you'll chop up.
And every morning, you'll make a fire.
And you'll be here outside my office in front.
And Arieh will be in the back.
Now, this oven I need to describe to you.
Because there were these means of heating the barracks in Auschwitz which were built.
Obviously, this was some sort of invention of the Germans for the army or something.
It wasn't meant as a-- the people who were designing it were not designing it for camp use.
They were designing it for other use.
So every one of these barracks had this long chimney horizontally through the barrack.
I calculate that the barrack held between 400 and 500 people.
And the barrack was about 100 meters long and perhaps 30 meters across-- you know, like 30 yards-- with a central area.
And this chimney was sort of about a meter wide and a meter high.
And it had two flues.
The flue from the front went horizontally through the whole barrack and came up out of the chimney.
And the chimney went through the roof on the back of the barrack.
And on the back, there was another place to make a fire.
And the flue would come forward.
And the chimney was in the front of the barrack.
Am I describing this well enough?
And the counterflow of the smoke of the-- from this boiler where you were stroking the fire was meant to heat the whole of the barrack.
And actually, the remarkable thing was that it worked.
I mean, what doesn't work which is German?
But it's very difficult to get it going.
Because you can only get the draft going once the flue is warm.
And Arieh Edelstein had never been to a [NON-ENGLISH] camp.
So he didn't know how to make a campfire.
He-- he-- he was a little bit of a wimp, like I said.
I mean, his father was a great man.
But the son was-- well, he was younger as well, a kid.
So I had the job of making the fire in front and then running to Arieh.
And his fire always went out.
And if his fire went, mine wouldn't draw.
Anyway, got the fire going eventually.
And there would these-- it was a jugendheim Freddy arranged, in this godforsaken place, he somehow got permission to get all the small children into a jugendheim.
And he did in Auschwitz Birkenau in the family camp what he did in Theresienstadt.
He took the children into a space where the space wasn't prison.
They started drawing paintings on the wall.
And they were divided into groups.
And each group had a [NON-ENGLISH] And they were told stories.
And they were singing songs.
And there was a choir, activities.
And I was the stoker, OK.
